questions.q: What is the defining difference between a function and a procedure in OCR Exam Reference Language?
options: A function returns a single value; a procedure returns no value
A procedure can take parameters but a function cannot
A function runs faster than a procedure
A procedure is always recursive but a function is not
questions.explain: A function performs a task and returns a single value (usable in an expression), whereas a procedure performs a task but returns no value.

questions.q: A variable is declared inside a subroutine. What is its scope?
options: Global — visible anywhere in the program
Local — visible only within that subroutine
Visible to the subroutine and any subroutine it calls
Visible only after the program ends
questions.explain: A variable declared inside a subroutine is local: it is visible only within that subroutine and its memory is freed when the subroutine exits.

questions.q: When an argument is passed BY VALUE, what happens to the original variable in the calling code if the subroutine changes its parameter?
options: The original is unchanged
The original is changed too
The program throws a stack overflow
The original becomes global
questions.explain: By value, a copy of the value is passed, so changes to the parameter do not affect the original variable — the original is unchanged.

questions.q: Which statement about passing BY REFERENCE is correct?
options: A copy of the data is passed, so the original is safe
The memory address is passed, so the original can be changed
It is always slower for large data structures
It cannot be used to return changes to the caller
questions.explain: By reference passes the memory address of the original, so the subroutine can change the original; this is efficient for large data and can return changes.

questions.q: A recursive subroutine is missing a reachable base case. What is the most likely result?
options: Infinite loop with no error
Stack overflow
Syntax error at compile time
The recursion silently returns zero
questions.explain: Without a reachable base case the subroutine keeps calling itself, pushing stack frames endlessly until the call stack is exhausted — a stack overflow.

questions.q: What does every correct recursive subroutine require?
options: A global variable and a loop
At least two parameters passed by reference
A base case and a general (recursive) case
A while loop inside the base case
questions.explain: Recursion needs a base case that stops further calls and a general/recursive case that calls itself while moving closer to the base case.

questions.q: On the call stack, in what order are stack frames removed as recursive calls return?
options: First in, first out (FIFO)
In order of parameter size
Randomly by the scheduler
Last in, first out (LIFO)
questions.explain: Each call pushes a frame holding parameters, locals and the return address; frames are popped in reverse order of being pushed — last in, first out.

questions.q: Compared with an equivalent iterative solution, recursion typically uses:
options: Less memory because only one set of variables exists
The same memory but always runs faster
More memory because each call adds a stack frame
No memory because frames are not stored
questions.explain: Each recursive call pushes a new stack frame, so recursion uses more memory than iteration, which keeps a single set of variables.

questions.q: Why is using local variables generally preferred over global variables?
options: They can be accessed from anywhere for convenience
They reduce side effects and make code more modular and easier to debug
They make the program run as a single module
They never need to be declared
questions.explain: Locals limit a variable to one subroutine, reducing unintended side effects, freeing memory on exit and keeping code modular, reusable and easier to debug.

questions.q: A local variable has the same name as a global variable. Inside that subroutine, which is used?
options: The global, because it was declared first
The local, which shadows the global within the subroutine
Both are merged into one value
Neither — this causes a syntax error
questions.explain: A local variable shadows a same-named global within its subroutine, so references inside the subroutine use the local value.

questions.q: Which OOP feature keeps attributes private and provides public methods to access them, protecting data integrity?
options: Inheritance
Polymorphism
Encapsulation
Overloading
questions.explain: Encapsulation bundles data and methods together and hides attributes by keeping them private, exposing controlled access through public getters/setters.

questions.q: A 'do...until' loop differs from a 'while' loop because it:
options: Tests the condition at the start, so may run zero times
Cannot be used for input validation
Tests the condition at the end, so always runs at least once
Repeats a fixed, known number of times
questions.explain: 'do...until' is post-conditioned: it tests at the end so the body always runs at least once, which suits input validation; 'while' tests at the start and may run zero times.

questions.q: Which of these problems is best described as non-computable?
options: Sorting a list of integers
The Halting Problem
Searching a sorted array
Calculating a factorial
questions.explain: The Halting Problem cannot be solved by any general algorithm, so it is non-computable; the others all have clear finite algorithmic solutions.

questions.q: A heuristic approach is best described as:
options: A method guaranteed to find the optimal solution
A way of breaking a problem into sub-problems
A technique for hiding unnecessary detail
A rule of thumb giving a good-enough answer quickly when an exact solution is too slow
questions.explain: A heuristic is a rule of thumb that yields a good-enough (not guaranteed optimal) answer quickly, used when an exact solution is too slow or impossible (e.g. A*, TSP).
